content.definition: Junctional rhythm originates from the AV junction rather than the sinus node. P waves may be absent, inverted, after the QRS or before the QRS with short PR.
Paced rhythm occurs when an artificial pacemaker depolarises atrial myocardium, ventricular myocardium or both.

content.text: Junctional and paced rhythms matter because rate alone is not enough. AV synchrony determines atrial contribution and ventricular filling, especially after valve surgery, RV failure, pulmonary hypertension and LV diastolic dysfunction.

items: Identify P-wave relationship: sinus, retrograde, absent or paced.
Assess rate, QRS width, AV relationship and arterial waveform response.
If junctional rhythm causes low output, consider atrial or AV sequential pacing if wires are present.
For paced rhythms, check mode, rate, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.
Escalate failure to capture, failure to sense, oversensing with pauses or hypotension.

content.text: Junctional rhythms
Junctional escape rhythm is usually 40-60/min. Accelerated junctional rhythm is 60-100/min. Junctional tachycardia is above 100/min. QRS is usually narrow unless aberrancy exists.
Why junctional rhythms occur after cardiac surgery
Causes include sinus node suppression, atrial oedema, atrial incision, valve surgery, ischaemia, hypothermia, electrolyte disturbance, digoxin, beta-blockers, amiodarone and catecholamines.
Loss of atrial contribution

In junctional rhythm, atrial activation may be retrograde or absent. Atria may contract against closed AV valves, raising venous pressure and reducing ventricular filling. This is poorly tolerated in stiff LV, AVR, mitral disease, RV dysfunction and LCOS.
Epicardial pacing overview
Temporary epicardial atrial and ventricular wires allow pacing and sensing after surgery. Wires exit the chest and connect to an external pacing box. Terminals must be insulated when not connected.
Atrial, ventricular and AV sequential pacing

rows: Stimulates atrium when AV conduction intact
AV block or failed atrial conduction.
Atrial then ventricular pacing after AV delay
Usual temporary support when sensing reliable.

content.text: Sensing and capture basics

Capture means output depolarises myocardium. Atrial capture produces a P wave after atrial spike; ventricular capture produces a wide QRS after ventricular spike. Sensing means the pacer detects intrinsic activity. Output is in mA. Sensitivity is in mV; lower mV means more sensitive.
Failure modes

Failure to capture is spike without P wave or QRS. Causes include low output, wire displacement, poor connection, battery failure, oedema, ischaemia, hyperkalaemia and acidosis. Undersensing causes inappropriate spikes despite intrinsic beats. Oversensing causes pacing inhibition and pauses.
Junctional rhythm at 70/min can still be low-output if atrial contraction is mistimed.
The arterial waveform is the truth test for pacing effectiveness.
Sensitivity is inverse: lower mV means more sensitive.
Never leave temporary epicardial wire terminals exposed.
ECG and monitor setup

For junctional and paced rhythms, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

Pacing boxes, epicardial wires and defibrillator pads are central safety equipment requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
May be tolerated or low-output depending on AV synchrony.
Can still reduce output if atrial contribution lost.
Lower mV is more sensitive; higher mV is less sensitive
Individualise to MAP, pulse pressure and lactate.

content.text: Escalate junctional rhythm with hypotension, raised CVP, low urine output or low output.
Escalate failure to capture, undersensing, oversensing, rising thresholds or pacing dependence.
Activate emergency response for pacing failure with shock, prolonged pauses, asystole or VT/VF.

quiz.question: Why can junctional rhythm reduce cardiac output at a normal-looking rate?
options: Atrial contraction may be absent or mistimed.
quiz.explanation: Loss of AV synchrony can reduce filling even when rate seems acceptable.

quiz.question: What is failure to capture?
options: Pacing spikes not followed by expected P wave or QRS.
No spikes because patient is in sinus rhythm.
quiz.explanation: The pacer fires but myocardium does not depolarise.

quiz.question: How do you make a pacer more sensitive?
quiz.explanation: Lower mV detects smaller intrinsic signals.
